text: The surgeon packs the small bowel into the upper abdomen, then inspects the anatomical landmarks and pathology within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a vessel-sealing and cutting instrument to mobilize the rectosigmoid colon from its left lateral attachment, carefully cutting through the fat while avoiding opening the retroperitoneum. The inferior pelvic ligament and ureter become visible during this process.

In the pelvic region, the surgeon encounters flimsy adhesions likely due to pelvic inflammatory disease, which are attached to the uterus. Small energy devices are used to carefully separate these adhesions.

The surgeon begins the hysterectomy by performing a salpingectomy, removing the fallopian tube. The utero-ovarian pedicle is exposed, identifying three structures: the round ligament, the utero-ovarian ligament, and the utero-ovarian vessels, which are noted for their limited vascularity.

The surgeon cuts the round ligament using minimal energy, followed by cutting the uterine ligament. The uterine vessels are then sealed using a multifunctional instrument, reducing the need for instrument exchanges and speeding up the procedure.

The surgeon identifies a small vessel extending to the round ligament and cuts it using a vessel sealing instrument. Adhesions between the appendix and the fallopian tube are also being cut. The surgeon performs a salpingectomy on the tube, staying close to it to avoid damaging the surrounding salpingial vessels and ovarian blood supply.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer instrument to manage thick vascular pedicles. The instrument is applied with pressure to ensure effective sealing before cutting. Once the tissue is sealed on both sides, the surgeon applies cutting current, pressure on the blade, and traction on the tissue to complete the cut.

The surgeon opens the broad ligaments using a V manipulator inside the uterus, performing lateral, anterior, and posterior dissection. The posterior peritoneum is reflected to the level of the intracellular attachment. The bladder dissection is challenging due to adhesions from PID and a previous cesarean, with minimal visible plane between the bladder and uterus.

The surgeon opens the broad ligament and moves laterally to identify the uterine vessels and the cervix below the bladder attachment at the scar level. The cervix, bladder, and uterine vessels are identified laterally, followed by de-peritonealization of the upper part.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel for bladder dissection in a patient with a previous cesarean section, focusing on opening the vault. The active lead of the scalpel is positioned upwards, and traction is applied using a grasper, requiring coordinated use of both hands in the laparoscopic procedure.

The surgeon opens the scar area, creating a lateral space that joins the center. Traction is applied using a manipulator and gasper on the uterus, revealing the uterine vessels and vein. The scar area is opened from the lateral side, releasing the bladder from the scar tissue. The bladder is lifted slightly, allowing some fibrotic tissue from the scar to remain on the bladder. The same procedure is performed on the right side, identifying the lateral window and creating space on the left side.

The surgeon releases the bladder laterally, with some cervical scar tissue visible on the bladder. The pink bladder tissue is identified, showing its vascularity. The cervix is seen laterally on both sides, with the bladder centrally adherent. Uterine vessels are clearly visible as the surgeon navigates between hard and soft tissue areas, reflecting scar tissue onto the bladder.

The surgeon completes dissection on both lateral sides and moves to the center, where the lower part shows minimal adhesions. The bladder is carefully pushed down into the vesicovaginal space, facilitating its release, which is crucial for proceeding with the hysterectomy in cases with a long cervix due to previous cesarean sections.

The surgeon lifts the bladder and enters the sacro-vaginal space, dissecting the bladder from the scar tissue. The bladder is visible being carefully separated, with the bend of the cervix and upper vagina also seen. The dissection reveals the bladder deeply embedded in scar tissue, making it challenging to find a clear plane for reflection.

The posterior leaf of the pericardium is reflected, revealing the attachment of the uterosacral ligament. The lateral dissection exposes the uterine vessels. The ultrasonic scalpel is used to seal and cut the uterine vessels, starting at the internal os level and lateralizing the stump to the uterosacral attachment.

The surgeon begins cutting at the internal os level, ensuring equal desiccation of tissues on both upper and lower sides. The incision is lateralized until reaching the attachment of the utero-sacral ligament, where the conics will open.

The surgeon begins cutting at the internal os level on the right side, lateralizing the uterine stump without entering the cervix. Both uterine structures are lateralized to the utero-sacral level, where the utero-sacral ligaments are clearly visible. The uterine manipulator is removed, and a myoma spiral is placed at the left upper quadrant of the uterus. The surgeon pulls the uterus towards the right port and uses an ultrasonic scalpel to open the vault posteriorly by detaching the utero-sacral ligaments.

The surgeon completes the circumferential incision around the vault without a uterine delineator. The vault closure is performed using continuous non-locking sutures with one-zero polyglactin, ensuring support by taking at least two sutures through the utero-sacral complex. The periservical ring is reconstructed with precise bites to minimize the risk of dehiscence.
The final view shows a small scar tissue on the bladder and a clean surgical field after the final wash.
